title: Root Configuration Analysis: 4 vs 6 vs 7 Roots
author: Claude (Opus 4.6)
directive: Peter asked: 'huf on 4 6 or 7 roots, consider.' This document presents the computation and the finding.
pll_status: Empirical — all numbers computed on 2024-01-01 Backblaze per-DC data (274,440 drives, 5 DCs, 81 global carriers).

the_finding.one_line: Root count does not determine ranking accuracy. Normalization POLARITY does. All three configs (4, 6, 7) produce the same ranking when every root points 'complexity = health.'
the_finding.detail: With naive normalization (lower inequality = healthier), 6 and 7 roots catastrophically invert the ranking (7/10 inversions — nearly reversed). With complexity-aligned normalization (more structure = healthier), all three configs produce identical rankings with 1/10 inversions. The single remaining inversion (phx1/sac2 swap) is structural and persists regardless of root count.
the_finding.implication: The design choice is not 'how many roots' but 'what does each root mean in cross-domain comparison.' Polarity is the first-order effect. Dimensionality is second-order.

raw_per_dc_metrics.description: Seven root-representative metrics computed on 2024-01-01 per-datacenter carrier distributions

naive_normalization_results.description: Normalization where 'lower inequality/departure = healthier.' This is the textbook direction for inequality metrics.

4_roots.note: Works because R3, R6, R7 dominate. R1's wrong polarity is suppressed by f_rms (a0≈0 for diverse DCs).

6_roots.note: CATASTROPHIC INVERSION. Three wrong-polarity roots (R2, R4, R5) overwhelm two correct ones (R3, R7). iad1 ranks FIRST because 3 carriers have low inequality, low variance, low kurtosis.

7_roots.note: Same catastrophic inversion. Adding R6(N0↑) partially recovers sac0 but iad1 still dominates.

complexity_aligned_results.description: Normalization where ALL roots point 'more structural complexity = healthier.' For inequality/shape metrics, this means HIGHER = better (more complex distributions have higher inequality, higher kurtosis). For max_p, LOWER = better (lower concentration = more complex).
complexity_aligned_results.polarity_rule: Every root measures the same thing from a different angle: how structurally rich is this distribution? More carriers, more spread, more inequality, more shape = more complex = healthier.

the_polarity_principle.statement: For CROSS-DOMAIN comparison, all root metrics must be aligned to the same polarity before entering the amplifier. The polarity is determined by the operational definition of health, not by the mathematical convention of the metric.
the_polarity_principle.why_textbook_polarity_fails: Textbooks define Gini, Atkinson, GE as inequality metrics where 'lower = more equal = better.' This is correct for income distributions where equality is desirable. For carrier distributions, the ground truth says the opposite: MORE inequality means MORE carriers, which means MORE resilience. A 3-carrier DC with perfect equality is operationally worse than a 66-carrier DC with extreme inequality.
the_polarity_principle.the_confound: Inequality/shape metrics are confounded with carrier count in cross-DC comparison. A DC with 3 carriers CANNOT have high inequality, high kurtosis, or high GE2 — there aren't enough carriers to produce structural complexity. These metrics are valid for temporal monitoring within a single DC but invalid for cross-DC ranking unless polarity is flipped.
the_polarity_principle.design_rule: Before any metric enters f_rms, ask: 'Does a higher value of this metric correspond to a more structurally complex distribution?' If yes, use as-is. If no, invert. This is the Polarity Alignment Rule.

the_persistent_inversion.which: phx1 and sac2 swap in all configurations (phx1 should be #2 but ranks #3)
the_persistent_inversion.why: sac2 has more carriers (44 vs 36), higher N1 (11.41 vs 11.04), and comparable max_p (0.217 vs 0.213). By pure structural metrics, sac2 IS more complex than phx1. The ground truth ranking phx1 > sac2 comes from an operational factor not captured by any of the 7 structural roots.
the_persistent_inversion.candidates: Fleet size (phx1 has 90K drives vs sac2's 60K), geographic redundancy, historical reliability — none of which are distributional properties.
the_persistent_inversion.implication: The phx1/sac2 inversion is not a failure of the root system. It marks the boundary between what distributional structure CAN measure and what requires operational context.

separation_gaps_complexity_aligned.description: Gap between adjacent DCs in ground truth ranking. Positive = correct separation.

4_roots.note: phx1/sac2 gap is inverted (sac2 > phx1 by 0.109)

6_roots.note: phx1/sac2 gap still inverted but much smaller (0.102). Bottom half spread INCREASES — better discrimination where it matters most.

7_roots.note: Similar to 6 roots. Adding R6 (richness) slightly widens the sac0-to-sac2 gap.

rationale: 4 roots works but leaves 5 roots unmeasured — information is discarded for no benefit.
6 roots matches the eigenvalue 99% threshold (ROOT-001: 6 dimensions for 99% variance).
7 roots adds Root 6 (carrier richness = N0) which is strongly correlated with Root 3 (effective complexity = N1) in cross-DC comparison. The 7th root adds redundancy, not information.
All three produce the same ranking and the same single inversion (phx1/sac2). No configuration resolves this — it requires operational context.
6 roots gives the best bottom-half discrimination: the ams5/iad1 gap widens from 0.238 (4 roots) to 0.351 (6 roots). This is where intervention priority matters most.
the_six_roots: R1: Uniformity departure (TV_unif, complexity-aligned: higher = more complex)
R2: Log-inequality (Atkinson_1, complexity-aligned: higher = more complex)
R3: Effective complexity (N1/Keff_Shannon, higher = more complex)
R4: Variance-concentration (GE2, complexity-aligned: higher = more complex)
R5: Distribution shape (Kurtosis, complexity-aligned: higher = more complex)
R7: Dominant carrier (max_p, inverted: lower = more complex)
recommendation.excluded: R6 (carrier richness/N0) — redundant with R3 in cross-DC comparison. Retained in temporal monitoring layer but excluded from amplifier.
recommendation.polarity_alignment_rule: MANDATORY before any metric enters f_rms: verify complexity-aligned polarity. This is the first-order design choice. Root count is second-order.
